 {"id":"bd-1ry","title":"Impl: WebAssembly bridge for PiJS (QuickJS has no wasm)","description":"# Assumption / Constraint
Assume **QuickJS does not provide WebAssembly** (`globalThis.WebAssembly` absent). We still must run the full pinned sample set, including extensions that ship Emscripten-style JS+WASM bundles.

# Goal
Provide a **PiWasm bridge** so JS-tier extensions can use a **WebAssembly-compatible API** even though the JS engine lacks wasm.

# Genericity constraint (non-negotiable)
- **No per-extension exceptions**: the bridge must not branch on extension id/name.
- Behavior is determined only by: wasm bytes, import object shape, policy/capabilities, and deterministic runtime configuration.
- Sample set is only used to define the **minimum required semantics**, not to justify one-off hacks.

# Strategy (hybrid)
- Use **wasmtime** (same engine as Tier A WASM host) to execute wasm.
- Expose a minimal `globalThis.WebAssembly` polyfill (or equivalent injected module) that is **generically compatible** with common JS+WASM glue patterns.
- All imports that represent side effects must route through **connector hostcalls** and therefore capabilities/logging.

# Scope / Deliverables
## WebAssembly JS surface (subset)
- `WebAssembly.instantiate(bytes, imports)`
- `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(resp, imports)` (if needed; otherwise extc rewrite to non-streaming)
- `WebAssembly.compile(bytes)` / `WebAssembly.Module`
- `WebAssembly.Instance` exports behavior used by typical glue code
- `WebAssembly.Memory` with `.buffer` semantics compatible with standard glue patterns
- Traps/errors mapped to actionable Pi errors + structured logs

## Import object handling
- Support JS functions as wasm imports with deterministic call ordering.
- Restrict / gate imports that imply OS access.

## Caching + perf
- Cache compiled wasm modules keyed by sha256(bytes + wasmtime_version + policy_version).
- Minimize cross-boundary copies; measure and optimize boundary overhead.

# Security constraints
- Enforce memory/table limits per extension policy.
- Ensure wasm cannot access host OS except via explicit connector imports.
- Structured logs: every instantiate/compile includes extension_id + artifact_id + elapsed + limits.

# Acceptance (hard)
- Any **wasm-using JS-tier artifact** in the pinned sample set runs end-to-end **without manual source edits** using this bridge (or Tier A when appropriate).
- The E2E harness passes **16/16** sample extensions.
- The implementation remains **generic** (no extension-id special-casing), enforced by tests/review.

# Tests (required)
- Unit tests: wasm compile/instantiate, memory growth boundaries, import call routing, trap mapping.
- Conformance fixtures for wasm-in-js scenarios with deterministic traces.
- Security tests: denied connector imports, exceeded limits, malicious wasm.

# Dependencies
- Must compose with unified capability model (bd-2hr) and dispatcher enforcement (bd-h04).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
- [ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead
- [ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
- [ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
- [ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
- [ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes

 {"id":"bd-1u0c","title":"Workstream: /share + /copy Parity (gist + clipboard)","description":"# Goal
Match legacy `/copy` and `/share` behaviors in interactive mode.

# Legacy Behavior
## `/copy`
- Copies the last assistant message to clipboard.
- If nothing to copy: show an error.

## `/share`
Legacy implementation (`legacy .../interactive-mode.ts`):
1) Ensure `gh` is installed and logged in (`gh auth status`).
2) Export session to a temp HTML file.
3) Show a cancellable loader UI while creating a **secret gist**:
   - `gh gist create --public=false <tmpFile>`
4) Parse gist URL from stdout and extract gist ID.
5) Compute viewer URL via `PI_SHARE_VIEWER_URL` (default `https://buildwithpi.ai/session/`) and show:
   - `Share URL: <viewer>#<gistId>`
   - `Gist: <gistUrl>`

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `/copy` is feature-gated and currently just reports “clipboard not enabled”.
- `/share` only writes an HTML file to temp and prints the path; no gist.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Implement cross-platform clipboard copy for `/copy` with actionable error messages.
- Implement gist-based `/share` with:
  - `gh` presence/auth checks
  - cancellable UI/loader
  - robust gist ID parsing
  - env var `PI_SHARE_VIEWER_URL` support
  - cleanup of temp files

# Testing
- Add tests that validate:
  - gist ID parsing
  - error messaging when `gh` missing or not logged in
  - `/copy` behavior with and without clipboard support (mocking where needed)

# Dependencies
- Keybinding layer is helpful for cancellation UX but not required.

## Acceptance Criteria
[ ] /copy copies last assistant message to clipboard
[ ] /share creates private gist via gh and prints viewer URL
[ ] PI_SHARE_VIEWER_URL supported
[ ] Share operation is cancellable and cleans up temp files

 {"id":"bd-1u4t","title":"Task: Implement UI Hostcall Handler (pi.ui)","description":"# Task: Implement UI Hostcall Handler (pi.ui)

## Objective

Implement the handler for HostcallKind::Ui that routes pi.ui() calls to TUI/RPC layer for user interaction.

## Background

Extensions can present UI to users via pi.ui:
- showSpinner(message): Display loading spinner with handle for update/stop
- showProgress(total, message): Display progress bar with handle
- showNotification(message, type): Display info/warn/error notification
- setStatusLine(text): Update status line
- askUser(prompt, options): Async prompt for user input
- select(options): Dropdown selection
- confirm(options): Yes/no confirmation
- input(options): Text input

## TypeScript Reference (extensions/src/ui.ts)

```typescript
interface ExtensionUI {
    showSpinner(message: string): SpinnerHandle;
    showProgress(total: number, message?: string): ProgressHandle;
    showNotification(message: string, type?: 'info' | 'warn' | 'error'): void;
    setStatusLine(text: string): void;
    askUser(prompt: string, options?: AskOptions): Promise<string>;
    select(options: SelectOptions): Promise<string | undefined>;
    confirm(options: ConfirmOptions): Promise<boolean>;
    input(options: InputOptions): Promise<string | undefined>;
}
```

## Implementation

### 1. UiContext Trait (src/extensions/ui_context.rs)

```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait UiContext: Send + Sync {
    fn show_spinner(&self, message: &str) -> Result<String>;  // Returns handle ID
    fn update_spinner(&self, id: &str, message: &str) -> Result<()>;
    fn stop_spinner(&self, id: &str) -> Result<()>;
    
    fn show_progress(&self, total: u64, message: Option<&str>) -> Result<String>;
    fn increment_progress(&self, id: &str, amount: u64) -> Result<()>;
    fn finish_progress(&self, id: &str) -> Result<()>;
    
    fn show_notification(&self, message: &str, notification_type: NotificationType) -> Result<()>;
    fn set_status_line(&self, text: &str) -> Result<()>;
    fn clear_status_line(&self) -> Result<()>;
    
    async fn ask_user(&self, prompt: &str, options: AskOptions) -> Result<String>;
    async fn select(&self, options: SelectOptions) -> Result<Option<String>>;
    async fn confirm(&self, options: ConfirmOptions) -> Result<bool>;
    async fn input(&self, options: InputOptions) -> Result<Option<String>>;
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum NotificationType { Info, Warning, Error }

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
pub struct AskOptions {
    pub prompt_type: PromptType,
    pub default: Option<String>,
    pub choices: Vec<String>,
}
```

### 2. TUI Implementation (src/tui/extension_ui.rs)

```rust
pub struct TuiUiContext {
    cmd_tx: tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender<Cmd>,
    spinners: Arc<RwLock<HashMap<String, String>>>,
    progress_bars: Arc<RwLock<HashMap<String, (u64, u64)>>>,
}

impl UiContext for TuiUiContext {
    fn show_spinner(&self, message: &str) -> Result<String> {
        let id = Uuid::new_v4().to_string();
        self.spinners.write().unwrap().insert(id.clone(), message.to_string());
        let _ = self.cmd_tx.blocking_send(Cmd::Custom(Box::new(
            ExtensionUiCmd::ShowSpinner { id: id.clone(), message: message.to_string() }
        )));
        Ok(id)
    }
    // ... other implementations
}
```

### 3. Dispatch Handler

```rust
impl HostcallDispatcher {
    async fn dispatch_ui(&self, method: &str, args: Value) -> Result<HostcallResponse> {
        let ui = self.ui_context.as_ref().ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("No UI context bound"))?;
        
        match method {
            "showSpinner" => {
                let message = args.get("message").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).unwrap_or("Loading...");
                let id = ui.show_spinner(message)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!({ "id": id })))
            }
            "updateSpinner" => {
                let id = args.get("id").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'id'"))?;
                let message = args.get("message").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'message'"))?;
                ui.update_spinner(id, message)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "stopSpinner" => {
                let id = args.get("id").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'id'"))?;
                ui.stop_spinner(id)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "showProgress" => {
                let total = args.get("total").and_then(|v| v.as_u64()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'total'"))?;
                let message = args.get("message").and_then(|v| v.as_str());
                let id = ui.show_progress(total, message)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!({ "id": id })))
            }
            "incrementProgress" => {
                let id = args.get("id").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'id'"))?;
                let amount = args.get("amount").and_then(|v| v.as_u64()).unwrap_or(1);
                ui.increment_progress(id, amount)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "finishProgress" => {
                let id = args.get("id").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'id'"))?;
                ui.finish_progress(id)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "showNotification" => {
                let message = args.get("message").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'message'"))?;
                let notification_type = match args.get("type").and_then(|v| v.as_str()) {
                    Some("warn") | Some("warning") => NotificationType::Warning,
                    Some("error") => NotificationType::Error,
                    _ => NotificationType::Info,
                };
                ui.show_notification(message, notification_type)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "setStatusLine" => {
                let text = args.get("text").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'text'"))?;
                ui.set_status_line(text)?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "clearStatusLine" => {
                ui.clear_status_line()?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(null)))
            }
            "askUser" => {
                let prompt = args.get("prompt").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("Missing 'prompt'"))?;
                let options: AskOptions = serde_json::from_value(args.get("options").cloned().unwrap_or_default())?;
                let result = ui.ask_user(prompt, options).await?;
                Ok(HostcallResponse::success(json!(result)))
            }
            _ => Err(anyhow!("Unknown UI method: {}", method)),
        }
    }
```

-{"id":"bd-1yo9","title":"Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution","description":"# Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution

## Overview

Extensions can register custom tools via pi.registerTool(). These tools must be:
1. Added to the agent's tool registry
2. Included in the tools array sent to the provider
3. Executable when the model invokes them

## Current State

The JS-side registration exists:
- `pi.registerTool(config)` registers a tool definition
- `__pi_execute_tool(name, callId, input)` exists in PI_BRIDGE_JS
- Tool definitions stored in extension's tools map

What's missing:
- Rust-side collection of registered tools
- Integration with agent's ToolRegistry
- Execution flow from model tool call → JS handler → result

## Architecture

```
Extension calls pi.registerTool()
            │
            ▼
JS runtime stores tool definition
            │
            ▼
Agent collects tools from all extensions ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Tools added to ToolRegistry ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Model receives tools in API call
            │
            ▼
Model invokes extension tool
            │
            ▼
Agent routes to extension handler ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
JS __pi_execute_tool() called ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Result returned to model
```

## Implementation Requirements

### 1. Collect Registered Tools
After extension activation, query JS runtime for registered tools:
```rust
let ext_tools = runtime.get_registered_tools()?;
```

### 2. Create Tool Wrapper
Wrap each extension tool as an AgentTool:
```rust
struct ExtensionToolWrapper {
    name: String,
    label: String,
    description: String,
    parameters: serde_json::Value,
    runtime: Arc<PiJsRuntime>,
}

#[async_trait]
impl Tool for ExtensionToolWrapper {
    async fn execute(&self, call_id: &str, input: Value) -> Result<ToolOutput> {
        self.runtime.execute_extension_tool(&self.name, call_id, input).await
    }
}
```

### 3. Add to Registry
```rust
for tool_def in runtime.get_registered_tools()? {
    let wrapper = ExtensionToolWrapper::new(tool_def, runtime.clone());
    tool_registry.register(wrapper);
}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Extension tools appear in provider's tools array
- [ ] Model can invoke extension tools
- [ ] Tool execution routes to JS handler
- [ ] Results return to model correctly
- [ ] Errors handled properly","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.168079003Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.174614141Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yo9","depends_on_id":"bd-30zg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.174573255Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1yr1","title":"Implement cancellation budgets for extension handlers","description":"Use asupersync cancellation budgets to bound cleanup time for extensions.

## Background
Cancellation budgets ensure:
- Cleanup completes in bounded time
- No indefinite waits during shutdown
- Predictable Ctrl+C behavior

## Current State
- Extensions use simple cancellation flags
- No guarantee cleanup completes quickly
- Long-running handlers may delay shutdown

## Implementation
1. Define cancellation budgets per operation type:
   - Extension event handler: 5s
   - Tool execution cleanup: 30s
   - UI dialog cancellation: 1s
2. Apply budgets via Cx::with_budget()
3. Force-cancel if budget exceeded

## Budget Configuration
Could be configurable in settings:
```json
{
  "extension_budgets": {
    "event_handler_ms": 5000,
    "tool_cleanup_ms": 30000
  }
}
```

## Test Plan
- Unit test: slow handler cancelled at budget
- Integration test: Ctrl+C cleanup completes quickly

- src/config.rs (budget settings)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:04.320977250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:45.195635915Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yr1","depends_on_id":"bd-20lm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:04.331895885Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1yr1","depends_on_id":"bd-2vie","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:45.195599067Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2ouv","title":"Task: Integrate extension tools into agent ToolRegistry","description":"# Task: Integrate Extension Tools into Agent ToolRegistry

## Objective

After extensions are activated, collect all registered tools and add them to the agent's tool registry so they appear in API calls.

## Background

Extensions register tools via pi.registerTool(). The agent needs to:
1. Query extensions for registered tools after activation
2. Wrap each tool with ExtensionToolWrapper
3. Add wrappers to ToolRegistry
4. Ensure tools are included in provider calls

## Implementation

### In Agent Initialization (src/agent.rs)

```rust
impl Agent {
    async fn initialize_extension_tools(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        // Get JS runtime from extension manager
        let runtime = self.extension_manager.runtime();
        
        // Get all registered tools from extensions
        let ext_tool_defs = runtime.get_registered_tools().await?;
        
        // Wrap and register each tool
        for def in ext_tool_defs {
            let wrapper = ExtensionToolWrapper::new(def, runtime.clone());
            self.tool_registry.register(Box::new(wrapper));
            
            tracing::debug!(
                tool = %wrapper.name(),
                "Registered extension tool"
            );
        }
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Call Site

In the agent initialization sequence, after extensions are loaded and activated:

```rust
impl Agent {
    pub async fn new(config: AgentConfig) -> Result<Self> {
        let mut agent = Self {
            // ... fields ...
        };
        
        // Load and activate extensions
        agent.extension_manager.load_extensions().await?;
        agent.extension_manager.activate_all().await?;
        
        // Register extension tools  <-- NEW
        agent.initialize_extension_tools().await?;
        
        Ok(agent)
    }
}
```

## Tool Collection Timing

Extension tools must be collected AFTER activation because:
1. Extension activate() may register additional tools dynamically
2. Tools registered in activate() must be captured
3. activation is when the extension is "ready"

## ToolRegistry Interface

Ensure ToolRegistry supports dynamic registration:

```rust
impl ToolRegistry {
    /// Register a tool dynamically.
    pub fn register(&mut self, tool: Box<dyn Tool>) {
        let name = tool.name().to_string();
        self.tools.insert(name, tool);
    }
    
    /// Get all registered tools for API calls.
    pub fn all_tools(&self) -> Vec<&dyn Tool> {
        self.tools.values().map(|t| t.as_ref()).collect()
    }
}
```

## Testing

1. Unit test: No extension tools -> registry unchanged
2. Unit test: One extension tool -> added to registry
3. Unit test: Multiple extension tools -> all added
4. Unit test: Extension tool appears in all_tools()
5. Integration test: Extension tool included in API call

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-254u (get_registered_tools)
- Depends on: bd-3ja5 (ExtensionToolWrapper)
- Part of: bd-1yo9 (Extension Tool Registration feature)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Extension tools collected after activation
- [ ] Tools wrapped with ExtensionToolWrapper
- [ ] Tools added to ToolRegistry
- [ ] Tools appear in provider API calls

 {"id":"bd-2vie","title":"Wrap extension lifecycle in Cx::region() for structured concurrency","description":"Use asupersync structured concurrency to guarantee cleanup of extension resources.

## Current State
- Extensions use basic Cx but not region() for lifecycle management
- Potential for orphaned extension tasks or leaked event handlers
- No guaranteed quiescence on extension unload

## Background (asupersync)
Cx::region() provides:
- Guaranteed child task completion on scope exit
- No orphaned tasks (enforced by type system)
- Bounded cleanup with cancellation budgets

## Implementation
1. Wrap ExtensionManager lifetime in Cx::region()
2. All extension event dispatch spawns tasks owned by the region
3. On shutdown, region guarantees all handlers complete or cancel
4. Add cancellation budget for extension cleanup (e.g., 5s max)

## Test Plan
- Unit test: extension with slow cleanup completes within budget
- Unit test: region exit waits for in-flight event handlers
- Integration test: Ctrl+C during extension execution cleans up properly

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions.rs (wrap in region)
- src/extensions_js.rs (spawn tasks via region)
- src/agent.rs (ensure extension region lifecycle)

## Dependencies
